questions.question: A 68-year-old male post-op day 2 from abdominal surgery develops acute shortness of breath, tachycardia (HR 112), and hypoxia (SpO2 88%). What is the most likely complication?
options: Pulmonary embolism from immobility and surgical trauma
Aspiration pneumonia from improper NPO status
questions.explanation: Post-operative PE is common due to venous stasis from immobility, endothelial injury from surgery, and hypercoagulability post-op. Clinical presentation includes acute dyspnea, tachycardia, hypoxia, and sometimes chest pain.

questions.question: A patient with acute MI develops cardiogenic shock with a systolic BP of 70 mmHg and urine output of 0.2 mL/kg/hr. Which intervention should be prioritized?
options: Vasopressor support with dobutamine or norepinephrine to improve perfusion
High-dose ACE inhibitor to reduce afterload
questions.explanation: Cardiogenic shock requires improving cardiac contractility and systemic perfusion. Vasopressors like dobutamine (inotropic, vasodilating) or norepinephrine (inotropic, vasoconstricting) are needed. IV fluids may worsen pulmonary edema; diuretics can worsen hypotension.

questions.question: In managing a patient with acute decompensated heart failure and pulmonary edema, which diuretic class is most appropriate for rapid symptom relief?
questions.explanation: Loop diuretics are the most potent diuretics, inhibiting Na-K-2Cl cotransporter in the thick ascending limb. In acute decompensated HF with pulmonary edema, IV loop diuretics rapidly reduce fluid volume and relieve symptoms.

questions.question: A patient recovering from major surgery develops a fever of 39.2°C (102.6°F) on post-op day 3, with elevated WBC and tachycardia. Which post-operative complication is most likely?
questions.explanation: Post-op day 3 fever with elevated WBC suggests infection, typically surgical site infection (SSI). SSIs usually manifest 3-5 days post-op with fever, erythema, purulent drainage, and elevated WBC. Malignant hyperthermia occurs during/immediately after surgery.

questions.question: A 55-year-old patient on mechanical ventilation develops acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS). Which of the following characterizes ARDS?
options: PaO2/FiO2 ratio <300 with bilateral infiltrates not fully explained by cardiac causes
Unilateral infiltrate with hypoxic respiratory failure
Normal chest X-ray with severe hypoxia
questions.explanation: Berlin Definition of ARDS requires: 1) Timing (within 1 week of known insult), 2) Bilateral opacities on imaging not fully from effusion/collapse/nodules, 3) Respiratory failure not fully explained by cardiac failure, 4) PaO2/FiO2 <300. Moderate ARDS is 100-200, severe is <100.

questions.question: A patient develops deep vein thrombosis (DVT) in the left leg. Which nursing intervention is priority?
options: Immediate leg massage to break up the clot
Strict bed rest with leg elevation and anticoagulation therapy
Application of heat to promote vasodilation
questions.explanation: DVT management prioritizes preventing PE and further thrombosis. Interventions include bed rest, elevation to reduce swelling and venous stasis, and anticoagulation (usually heparin then warfarin). Massage can dislodge thrombus; ambulation and heat can worsen outcome.

questions.question: A patient post-operatively develops acute urine retention. Which medication commonly causes this side effect?
questions.explanation: Opioids are notorious for causing post-operative urinary retention by decreasing detrusor (bladder muscle) contractility and increasing urethral sphincter tone. This is a known side effect of opioid analgesia commonly used for post-op pain.

questions.question: In a patient with COPD exacerbation presenting with respiratory acidosis (pH 7.25, PaCO2 60), what is the immediate priority?
options: Administer sodium bicarbonate IV to correct acidosis
Improve ventilation with bronchodilators, oxygen, and possibly non-invasive or mechanical ventilation
Administer acetazolamide to increase CO2 elimination
questions.explanation: Respiratory acidosis in COPD is caused by CO2 retention from inadequate ventilation. Treatment focuses on improving ventilation (not correcting pH directly). Bronchodilators, corticosteroids, oxygen, and possibly BiPAP/intubation are needed. Bicarb and acetazolamide are not appropriate.

questions.question: A patient receives IV potassium chloride (KCl) infusion and develops peaked T-waves on ECG. What is the immediate action?
options: Continue current infusion rate; peaked T-waves are expected with K replacement
STOP infusion; give calcium gluconate for membrane stabilization and insulin with dextrose to shift K into cells
Increase infusion rate to achieve goal K+ faster
questions.explanation: Peaked T-waves are a sign of hyperkalemia (ECG changes: peaked T, widened QRS, prolonged PR, ST depression). This is dangerous and can lead to cardiac arrhythmias. Immediate treatment: stop K+ infusion, give calcium gluconate (cardiac membrane stabilization), insulin + dextrose (K+ shift), consider diuretics or dialysis.

questions.question: A 72-year-old female with recent hip fracture develops confusion, agitation, and hallucinations on post-op day 1. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute delirium from multiple factors (pain, medications, infection, hypoxia, immobility)
questions.explanation: Post-operative delirium is acute, fluctuating confusion common in elderly post-op patients. Causes include pain, opioids/sedatives, infection, hypoxia, electrolyte imbalance, urinary/fecal retention, and immobility. Distinguishing features: acute onset, fluctuating course, inattention.
